currentUse: Home of The Abundant Life Fellowship Ministries, Inc., a non-Catholic Christian congregation (Pastor Ulysses J. Youngblood, Sr.), at 195-199 North Main Street, Ansonia, CT; Sunday service 12:30 pm
summary: The "unnamed" Lithuanian church of the archive record is identifiable as St. Anthony's (Sv. Antano) Lithuanian parish at 199 North Main Street, Ansonia, CT — founded by Lithuanians who incorporated a Lithuanian Society of St. Anthony lodge in 1907, began building without episcopal approval in 1912 after Bishop John J. Nilan refused a parish, won papal sanction on appeal to Rome in 1915, and dedicated the church on Thanksgiving Day 1915. The parish celebrated its 100th anniversary Mass on November 22, 2015 and closed, with a final Mass reported on November 29, 2015. The building still stands and remains in religious use: it is now occupied by The Abundant Life Fellowship Ministries, Inc., a non-Catholic Christian congregation (originally founded in Bridgeport in 1979 as House of Prayer and Deliverance, re-established under Pastor Ulysses J. Youngblood, Sr. in 2010), which states it purchased the two-corner-lot property at 195-199 North Main Street and holds Sunday services there at 12:30 pm. A Lithuanian monument bearing the Columns of Gediminas — the remnant of a large chapel-post by Kestutis Svelnys whose top was destroyed by termites — still stands in the former churchyard, surrounded by a fence with Lithuanian ethnic motifs and dedicated to the parish founders, benefactors, the persecuted church in Soviet-occupied Lithuania, and the Lithuanian nation. No published sale price, buyer transaction record, or recent press coverage, preservation effort, or reunion activity was found.

developments.headline: St. Anthony's Lithuanian parish closes after centennial
developments.detail: The parish marked its 100th anniversary with a Mass on November 22, 2015, and closed; a reader comment on Historic Buildings of Connecticut reports the final celebration of Mass on November 29, 2015. Global True Lithuania likewise records the church as closed in 2015 after its centennial.

developments.headline: Building passes to Abundant Life Fellowship Ministries
developments.detail: Global True Lithuania reports the closed Lithuanian church became an 'Abundant Life' church. The Abundant Life Fellowship Ministries, Inc. — a congregation founded in Bridgeport in 1979 as House of Prayer and Deliverance and re-established under Pastor Ulysses J. Youngblood, Sr. in 2010 — states on its website that it purchased and relocated to the two-corner-lot property at 195-199 North Main Street, Ansonia, matching St. Anthony's address of 199 N Main St. Exact purchase date and price were not found in published sources.

sources.title: Church of St. Anthony, Ansonia, Connecticut, USA

developments.headline: Gediminas-columns monument survives in the former churchyard
developments.detail: A Lithuanian monument adorned with the Columns of Gediminas still stands in the yard of the former church. It is the remnant of one of Connecticut's massive chapel-posts, designed by Kestutis Svelnys; its top was destroyed by termites, and the surviving portion, surrounded by a fence with Lithuanian ethnic motifs, carries dedications to the St. Anthony parish founders, workers and benefactors, the persecuted church in Soviet-occupied Lithuania, the Lithuanian nation, and the USA.

sources.title: Church of St. Anthony, Ansonia, Connecticut, USA
The Abundant Life Fellowship Ministries, 195-199 N Main St, Ansonia

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Refinements, no contradictions. (1) The archive lists the parish as unnamed; sources identify it as St. Anthony's (Lithuanian) parish, 199 North Main Street — founded via a 1907 St. Anthony Society lodge, built from 1912 without episcopal approval, sanctioned by Rome in 1915, dedicated Thanksgiving Day 1915. (2) The archive's 'closed and sold ~2015 to non-Catholics' is confirmed and sharpened: centennial Mass November 22, 2015, final Mass reported November 29, 2015, and the building is now occupied by The Abundant Life Fellowship Ministries, a non-Catholic Protestant congregation that states it purchased the property. (3) The Gediminas-pillars monument in the churchyard is corroborated by Global True Lithuania, including its origin as a Kestutis Svelnys chapel-post whose top was lost to termites.
gaps: No published sale price, deed date, or transaction record was found (searched news, real-estate listings, and general web for the 195-199/199 North Main Street property; Ansonia land records are not searchable via open web). The exact year Abundant Life took occupancy is not stated anywhere found — only that it followed the 2010 leadership change and the 2015 closure. No diocesan (Archdiocese of Hartford) announcement of the closure/relinquishment was located online. No 2020s press, preservation efforts, reunions, or Lithuanian community activity around the site was found. The current physical condition of the Gediminas monument could not be verified more recently than the undated Global True Lithuania page (which reflects a site visit of unknown date). Founding (1907 society / 1915 parish sanction) and closing (1915-2015) dates come from a local-history blog and heritage site, not primary diocesan records.
